section.title: The Plot against Jeremiah
section.sectionRationale: Jeremiah discovers a murder plot from his own hometown, led like a lamb to slaughter without knowing it, and pours out to God the first of his confessions, why do the wicked prosper? God's own answer turns personal: He too has been forsaken by His beloved inheritance, yet even in decreeing uprooting He promises compassion and restoration to come.
section.thesis: Jeremiah's own townsmen conspire to kill him for faithfully speaking God's word, and his honest complaint to God about the prosperity of the wicked is met not with a simple answer but with God's own greater grief over a people who have forsaken Him, though even here uprooting is not God's last word, since compassion and restoration remain promised.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the murder plot against Jeremiah uncovered and judgment pronounced on the men of Anathoth; Jeremiah's honest complaint that the wicked prosper while he suffers for faithfulness; God's own lament that His beloved inheritance has forsaken Him and become like a lion roaring against Him; and a final word of hope, that even the nations uprooted for cruelty may yet be established among God's people if they turn to Him.

verses.text: And the LORD informed me, so I knew. Then You showed me their deeds.

For I was like a gentle {{kebes|lamb}} led to slaughter; I did not know that they had plotted against me: “Let us destroy the tree with its fruit; let us cut him off from the land of the living, 
that his name may be remembered no more.”
O LORD of Hosts, who judges righteously, who examines the heart and mind, let me see Your vengeance upon them, for to You I have committed my cause.
Therefore this is what the LORD says concerning the people of Anathoth who are seeking your life and saying, “You must not prophesy in the name of the LORD, or you will die by our hand.”
So this is what the LORD of Hosts says: “I will punish them. Their young men will die by the sword, their sons and daughters by famine.
There will be no remnant, for I will bring disaster on the people of Anathoth in the year of their punishment.”

units.label: Why does the way of the wicked prosper?

verses.text:
Righteous are You, O LORD, when I plead before You. Yet about Your judgments I wish to {{rib|contend}} with You: 
Why does the way of the wicked prosper? Why do all the faithless live at ease?
You planted them, and they have taken root. They have grown and produced fruit. You are ever on their lips, but far from their hearts.
But You know me, O LORD; You see me and test my heart toward You. Drag away the wicked like sheep to the slaughter and set them apart for the day of carnage.

How long will the land mourn and the grass of every field be withered? Because of the evil of its residents, the animals and birds have been swept away, for the people have said, “He cannot see what our end will be.”

“If you have raced with men on foot and they have worn you out, 
how can you compete with horses? If you stumble in a peaceful land, how will you do in the thickets of the Jordan?
Even your brothers, your own father’s household, even they have betrayed you; even they have cried aloud against you. Do not trust them, though they speak well of you.

units.label: I have forsaken My house
units.note: God's own grief over His inheritance

verses.text: I have forsaken My house; I have abandoned My {{nachalah|inheritance}}; I have given the love of My life into the hands of her enemies.
My inheritance has become to Me like a lion in the forest. She has roared against Me; therefore I hate her.

Is not My inheritance to Me like a speckled bird of prey 
with other birds of prey circling against her? Go, gather all the beasts of the field; bring them to devour her.
Many shepherds have destroyed My vineyard; they have trampled My plot of ground. They have turned My pleasant field into a desolate wasteland.
They have made it a desolation; desolate before Me, it mourns. All the land is laid waste, but no man takes it to heart.

Over all the barren heights in the wilderness the destroyers have come, for the sword of the LORD devours from one end of the earth to the other. 
No flesh has peace.
They have sown wheat but harvested thorns. They have exhausted themselves to no avail. Bear the shame of your harvest because of the fierce anger of the LORD.”

units.label: I will have compassion
units.note: uprooting is not the last word

verses.text: This is what the LORD says: “As for all My evil neighbors who attack the inheritance that I bequeathed to My people Israel, I am about to {{nathash|uproot}} them from their land, and I will uproot the house of Judah from among them.
But after I have uprooted them, I will once again have compassion on them and return each one to his inheritance and to his land.

And if they will diligently learn the ways of My people and swear by My name, saying, ‘As surely as the LORD lives’, just as they once taught My people to swear by Baal, then they will be established among My people.
But if they will not obey, then I will uproot that nation; I will uproot it and destroy it, declares the LORD.”

kebes.gloss: A young, gentle lamb. Jeremiah pictures himself led to slaughter without suspecting it, an image later fulfilled supremely in the suffering servant of Isaiah 53:7 and in Christ, the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world.

rib.gloss: To contend, to bring a legal dispute or lawsuit. Jeremiah uses courtroom language to bring his honest complaint about the prosperity of the wicked directly before God, modeling faith that argues with God rather than abandoning Him in silence.

nachalah.gloss: Inheritance, a possession given and kept within a family. God repeatedly calls Israel My inheritance in this passage, showing that even in judgment, the relationship of belonging is not simply erased but grieved over as a real loss.

nathash.gloss: To uproot, pluck up, or tear out, a word Jeremiah uses often for both Israel's judgment and the nations'. Strikingly, the same word that decrees uprooting here also frames the promise of compassion and restoration that follows it.

basic.context: Jeremiah learns that the men of his own hometown, Anathoth, have plotted to kill him for prophesying in the LORD's name, and he had not even suspected it, like a gentle lamb led to slaughter. He commits his cause to God and brings the first of his personal confessions recorded in this book, an honest complaint that the wicked prosper while the faithful suffer. God's answer does not simply explain the injustice; it reveals His own grief, since He too has been abandoned by the very people He calls His inheritance.
basic.hermeneutics: This is the first of several raw, personal laments Jeremiah brings directly to God through chapters 11 to 20, showing that honest complaint, even bordering on accusation, has a legitimate place within a life of faith when brought to God rather than nursed in bitterness apart from Him. God's response, in effect you have not seen anything yet, is less an explanation of theodicy than a call to deeper endurance grounded in God's own greater sorrow.
basic.originalLanguages: Jeremiah describes himself as a kebes, gentle lamb, led unknowingly to slaughter, an image later fulfilled in Christ. He tells God he wishes to rib, contend or bring a legal case, over the prosperity of the wicked. God answers by naming Judah His own nachalah, inheritance, now turned against Him, yet still decrees to nathash, uproot, and then, remarkably, promises to have compassion and restore.
basic.typology: Jeremiah as a lamb led to slaughter without knowing it anticipates Isaiah's suffering servant and finds its fullest fulfillment in Christ, the Lamb of God led to the cross. Jeremiah's honest legal complaint against apparent injustice anticipates the pattern of lament fulfilled and absorbed into the cross, where the righteous One suffers unjustly yet entrusts Himself to the Father's justice.
basic.christ: Jeremiah's description of himself as a lamb led to slaughter without knowing it is picked up by Isaiah's suffering servant, He was led like a lamb to the slaughter, Isaiah 53:7, and fulfilled in Jesus, the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world, John 1:29, who like Jeremiah faced a conspiracy of His own people yet entrusted Himself to the Father who judges justly, 1 Peter 2:23.
basic.god: God is not distant from His prophet's suffering; He examines the heart and mind and receives Jeremiah's raw complaint without rebuking his honesty. Yet the deeper revelation here is God's own anguish: He calls Israel the love of My life, given into her enemies' hands, showing that His judgment is never coldly administered but flows from a wounded covenant love. Even His decree to uproot ends with a promise of compassion for those who will turn to Him.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 53:7 pictures the servant as a lamb led to slaughter, the image Jeremiah first uses of himself. John 1:29 identifies Jesus as this Lamb. 1 Peter 2:23 describes Christ entrusting Himself to the Father who judges justly, exactly what Jeremiah does in 11:20. Habakkuk 1:2-4 voices the same complaint about the prosperity of the wicked.
basic.oneStory: This is the pattern of the righteous sufferer who cries out honestly to God amid undeserved persecution, a pattern running from Jeremiah through the Psalms of lament to its fullest expression in Christ, the true Lamb led to slaughter, who bore the world's injustice while trusting the Father's just judgment, and who secures the very compassion and restoration promised even to uprooted nations at the end of this passage.

context.literary: This section stands at the start of the ten chapter block, 11:1-20:18, that mixes narrative about Jeremiah's own life with poetic laments, and it introduces the pattern of personal confession that recurs through chapter 20.
context.historical: Anathoth, Jeremiah's hometown, was a priestly town in Benjamin; the plot against him may reflect resentment at his prophetic condemnation of the very religious establishment his own family and neighbors belonged to.
context.canonical: Jeremiah's confessions here begin a pattern of honest lament before God that echoes the Psalms and anticipates the pattern of unjust suffering entrusted to God's justice, fulfilled climactically in Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To record Jeremiah's genuine, unguarded complaint to God over persecution and the apparent prosperity of the wicked, and to show that God's response, rather than a simple explanation, calls the prophet to deeper endurance while revealing God's own grief over the very people causing the suffering.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This passage describes a specific historical plot against Jeremiah and his specific complaint about it; it is not a promise that every faithful person's persecutors will be punished exactly as Jeremiah requests. The enduring, prescriptive truth is that honest complaint belongs within faith, and that God's justice, though sometimes delayed, is never absent.

debates.question: Is Jeremiah's request for vengeance in 11:20 and 12:3 a model prayer for believers, or does it reflect an incomplete understanding later corrected by Christ?

views.label: A legitimate, faith filled appeal to God's justice
views.case: Jeremiah commits his cause to God rather than taking vengeance himself, asking God, the righteous judge, to act; this mirrors imprecatory psalms and is treated in Scripture as an appropriate cry for justice against genuine, murderous persecution.

views.label: An honest but limited prayer surpassed by Christ's example
views.case: Others note that Jesus prays for His persecutors' forgiveness even from the cross, Luke 23:34, suggesting the New Testament pattern moves believers further toward loving enemies even while still trusting God as the ultimate judge.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree Jeremiah's prayer is honest and appropriately directed to God rather than self-administered revenge; the debate concerns how this fits alongside the fuller revelation of enemy love shown in Christ, not whether such honesty before God is legitimate.

study.originalLanguages: Jeremiah pictures himself as a kebes, gentle lamb, led unknowingly to slaughter, language later applied directly to Christ. He asks to rib, contend, with God over the wicked's prosperity, using the same legal vocabulary found in covenant lawsuits elsewhere in the prophets. God answers by calling Israel His own nachalah, inheritance, a term of deep familial belonging, even while decreeing to nathash, uproot, both Judah and her hostile neighbors, a judgment that remarkably gives way to a promise of compassion and restoration.

typology.type: Jeremiah as a gentle lamb led to slaughter, unaware of the plot against him, Jeremiah 11:19
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Christ, the true Lamb of God, John 1:29, who like the suffering servant was led like a lamb to the slaughter, Isaiah 53:7.

typology.type: Jeremiah entrusting his cause to God rather than taking vengeance himself, Jeremiah 11:20
typology.fulfillment: Christ entrusts Himself to the Father who judges justly while suffering unjustly, 1 Peter 2:23.

study.christ: Jeremiah's description of himself as a lamb led to slaughter without knowing it is picked up directly by Isaiah's suffering servant, He was led like a lamb to the slaughter, Isaiah 53:7, and fulfilled in Jesus, the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world, John 1:29. Like Jeremiah, Jesus faced a conspiracy from among His own people, yet where Jeremiah asks God to see His vengeance upon his enemies, Jesus goes further still, entrusting Himself to the Father who judges justly, 1 Peter 2:23, and even praying for those who conspired against Him, Luke 23:34.
study.god: God is not distant from His prophet's suffering; He examines the heart and mind and receives Jeremiah's raw complaint without rebuking his honesty. Yet the deeper revelation here is God's own anguish over sin: He calls Israel the love of My life, given into her enemies' hands, showing His judgment is never coldly administered but flows from a wounded covenant love. Even His decree to uproot both Judah and her hostile neighbors ends with a promise of compassion for any who turn to Him.

crossReferences.note: Pictures the suffering servant as a lamb led to slaughter, the image Jeremiah first applies to himself.
Identifies Jesus directly as this Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world.
Describes Christ entrusting Himself to the Father who judges justly, exactly what Jeremiah does in 11:20.
Voices a strikingly similar complaint about the prosperity of the wicked and the silence of God.

study.oneStory: This is the pattern of the righteous sufferer who cries out honestly to God amid undeserved persecution, a pattern running from Jeremiah through the Psalms of lament to its fullest expression in Christ, the true Lamb led to slaughter, who bore the world's injustice while trusting the Father's just judgment, and who secures the very compassion and restoration promised even to uprooted nations at the end of this passage.

sermon.title: Christmas at the Castle, Part 3
sermon.why: Carson works through Jeremiah 11:18 to 12:17 in detail, reading Jeremiah's complaint about the prosperity of the wicked and God's response, exactly the confession and answer this study examines.

quiz.q: What does Jeremiah compare himself to in 11:19, describing his ignorance of the plot against him?

options.text: A gentle lamb led to slaughter, not knowing they had plotted against him.
options.note: Verse 19 states this image directly.

options.text: A lion protecting its territory from attackers.
options.note: This is the opposite image; Jeremiah pictures himself as vulnerable and unsuspecting, not fierce.

options.text: A soldier armed and ready for battle.
options.note: This contradicts the picture of unsuspecting vulnerability the text actually describes.

options.text: A shepherd searching for a lost sheep.
options.note: Not the image used; Jeremiah is the vulnerable one being led away, not the one searching.

quiz.q: What specific complaint does Jeremiah bring to God in 12:1?

options.text: Why does the way of the wicked prosper, and why do all the faithless live at ease?
options.note: Verse 1 states this complaint directly.

options.text: Why did God allow the temple to be built in the first place?
options.note: Not the complaint raised in this verse; the temple is not the subject here.

options.text: Why did God choose him rather than someone else as prophet?
options.note: Jeremiah questions his call elsewhere, but that is not this specific complaint.

options.text: Why does the land continue to suffer drought?
options.note: Drought becomes the subject later in chapters 14-15, not the complaint raised here.

quiz.q: What does God call Judah in 12:7, expressing His own grief?

options.text: My house, My inheritance, the love of My life given into her enemies' hands.
options.note: Verse 7 uses exactly this intimate, grieving language.

options.text: An enemy He had always opposed from the beginning.
options.note: The text expresses grief over betrayed love, not original enmity.

options.text: A distant nation with no prior covenant relationship.
options.note: This contradicts the entire premise of covenant intimacy running through this passage.

options.text: A stranger who had never truly belonged to Him.
options.note: This contradicts the possessive, intimate language of verse 7, My house, My inheritance.

quiz.q: Who does God say has betrayed Jeremiah, even close to home, according to 12:6?

options.text: Even his own brothers and his father's household have betrayed him and cried aloud against him.
options.note: Verse 6 states this plainly.

options.text: No one at all; Jeremiah faced no betrayal from anyone.
options.note: This directly contradicts the passage's account of betrayal.

options.text: Foreign kings who sought to conquer Judah.
options.note: The betrayal described in this verse comes from close family, not foreign powers.

options.text: Only distant strangers from neighboring towns.
options.note: The text specifically names Jeremiah's own brothers and household, not distant strangers.

quiz.q: What hope does God hold out even for the wicked neighboring nations in 12:14-17?

options.text: If they diligently learn the ways of His people and swear by His name, they will be established among His people.
options.note: Verse 16 states this conditional hope plainly.

options.text: That God has no further dealings with any nation beyond Israel.
options.note: This contradicts the passage, which explicitly addresses these surrounding nations directly.

options.text: That they will be destroyed with no possibility of restoration whatsoever.
options.note: This contradicts verses 15 to 16, which hold out compassion and possible restoration.

options.text: That they will permanently inherit the land of Judah.
options.note: The passage describes uprooting and possible restoration to their own land, not permanent inheritance of Judah's territory.

questions: Jeremiah did not even suspect that his own townspeople were plotting against him. Has faithfulness to God ever cost you a relationship you did not expect to lose?

Jeremiah brought his honest complaint, why do the wicked prosper, directly to God rather than suppressing it. Where do you need to bring an honest complaint to God instead of masking it with false peace?
God says His own heart grieves over Judah, His beloved inheritance turned against Him. How does knowing God feels real grief over sin change the way you picture Him this week?

God's answer to Jeremiah's complaint was, in effect, you have not seen anything yet, calling him to deeper endurance rather than an easy explanation. How do you tend to respond when God's answer to your prayers is not the explanation you wanted?
Even here, God promises compassion and restoration to nations who turn to Him. Where do you need to trust that God's compassion can reach further than you currently believe?

translationNotes.note: The gentle lamb image directly anticipates Isaiah 53:7's servant song, later applied explicitly to Christ in Acts 8:32 and John 1:29.
The verb rendered contend is the same legal term used for covenant lawsuits elsewhere in the prophets, framing Jeremiah's complaint as a formal appeal to God's own justice.
The Hebrew behind speckled bird of prey is genuinely obscure and variously rendered across translations, but every reading pictures Judah as marked out for attack by surrounding predators.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the Makor scripture toolkit and sermon link verified via live web fetch this session confirming Don Carson's sermon covering Jeremiah 11 to 15 at thegospelcoalition.org.
